These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for recieved tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the trasnport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: MSW landfilling of disodium octaborate tetrahydrate-treated wood; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs as well as gas and leachate emissions from disposal of DOT-treated wood products in an MSW landfill environment.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care.  A significant fraction of methane produced from wood decomposition is captured and combusted to carbon dioxide at MSW landfills with a gas collection and control system (GCCS). The methane and biogenic carbon dioxide emission from landfills with a GCCS would be lower and higher, respectively, than from landfills without GCCS. The US EPA (2012) estimated that the temporally-averaged gas collection efficiency for lumber and MDF ranges from 76% to 92%.  Using the average nationwide statistics for the percentage of landfills that have GCCS and assuming a 90% average gas collection efficiency as provided for dimensional lumber in US EPA (2012b), the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of wood  in an MSW landfill are estimated to be 0.022 kg, and 0.33 kg, respectively. Over the 40-year gas collection period simulated by this dataset, wood treatment chemicals were assumed to not have an impact on total gas generation from this treated wood product. Leachate emissions are estimated based on TCLP measurements by Dubey et al. (2010).

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of wood products at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of treated wood products as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions  calculated from Waste Business Journal database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: MSW landfilling of fiberglass insulation; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models gas emissions, leachate volume emissions, materials and energy inputs, and equipment emissions from the placement of fiberglass insulation in a municipal solid waste (MSW) landfill, using a calculated density of the average compacted fiberglass insulation product.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with MSW landfill operation, closure and post-closure care.  Due to an absence of TCLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an MSW landfill environment), leachate emissions from the MSW landfilling of this material are not included in this dataset.

Gas emissions were estimated assuming the decomposition of the kraft paper facing used in faced fiberglass insulation.  The mass of kraft paper facing used in the average discarded US fiberglass insulation product was calculated using 2002 economic census data (USCB 2005) on the total sales value of major categories of fiberglass insulation products. The US EPA (2012b) estimated that the temporally-averaged gas collection efficiency for corrugated containers (used as a surrogate for kraft paper) ranges from 81-91%.  Using the average nationwide statistics for the percentage of landfills that have a GCCS and assuming a 89% average gas collection efficiency as provided for corrugated containers in US EPA (2012) the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of fiberglass insulation in an MSW landfill are estimated to be 8.2 grams, and 0.57 grams, respectively.

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the calculated, compacted density of fiberglass insulation with the compression factor calculated in IWCS (2017).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Processing of land clearing debris; mulched; at processing facility
Description: This process dataset accounts for the emissions and other environmental burdens associated with the operation of a land clearing debris (LCD) processing facility per kilogram of throughput. It accounts for diesel and heavy equipment utilization, particulate matter (PM) emissions associated with stockpiling and unpaved road transport, electricity consumption, water consumption (for PM control from LCD grinding), and total hydrocarbon and methanol emissions from LCD grinding. Diesel consumption and heavy equipment utilization are based on information reported by Springsteen et al. (2011) for operation of an LCD processing operation to produce a biomass fuel end product.

Description: A gate-to-gate process modeling recycling residential carpet at a U.S. carpet recycling facility.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Reuse of clay brick; at construction
Description: This process dataset accounts for the reuse of clay bricks for new construction. This process includes transport from a demolition site to the reuse site, as well as the generation of mortar and other non-reusable waste due to preparing clay bricks for reuse. This process does not account for any other emissions that would result from cleaning or preparing waste clay bricks for reuse.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Sludge hauling and landfill; wastewater treatment unit; Level 1
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects landfill disposal of biosolids generated during the treatment of 1 cubic meter of wastewater by the (linked) conventional plug flow activated sludge, Level 1 treatment system. Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the disposal process include biosolids transport, basic infrastructure required for sludge storage at the WWTP, landfill fugitive methane emissions, emissions of toxic organics and heavy metals in landfill leachate,  and the option to include an avoided electricity benefit associated with energy recovery of landfill gas.

Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the disposal process include biosolids transport, basic infrastructure required for sludge storage at the WWTP, landfill fugitive methane emissions, emissions of toxic organics and heavy metals in landfill leachate,  and the option to include an avoided electricity benefit associated with energy recovery of landfill gas.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of alkaline copper quaternary-treated wood; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the degradation of Alkaline Copper Quaternary (ACQ)-treated wood in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

As estimated using the carbon content, moisture content, and cover soil oxidation values provided in US EPA (2012b), the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of wood  in an CDD landfill are estimated to be 0.064 kg, and 0.21 kg, respectively. Wood treatment chemicals were assumed not to have an impact on gas generation. Leachate emissions are estimated based on SPLP measurements by Dubey et al. (2010).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of treated wood as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of copper wire; 12-2; with ground; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of 12-2 (with ground) copper building wire in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill using the density of copper wire as calculated from Cerrowire (2013).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

Because the specific copper wire modeled by this dataset incorporates a paper sheathing, it is possible that the landfill disposal of this material may contribute to landfill gas emissions. However, because the paper sheathing is located within the outer PVC jacketing and because details were not found on the mass fraction of paper per length of 12-2 copper wire, landfill gas production is not included in this dataset. Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment), leachate emissions from the CDD landfilling of this material are not included in this dataset. However, it should be noted that copper wire PVC insulation and jacketing may contain phthalate-based plasticizers and lead (used as a heat stabilizer) and these components could potentially leach out of the insulation and jacketing in a landfill environment (US EPA, 2008).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of copper wire, calculated from the information presented in Cerrowire (2013), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of fiberglass insulation; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models gas emissions, leachate emissions, materials and energy inputs, and equipment emissions from the placement of fiberglass insulation in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill, using a calculated density of the average compacted fiberglass insulation product.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment) specific to fiberglass insulation, studies quantifying the leachate emissions from the individual layers of fiberglass insulation (i.e., fiberglass batt, kraft paper, asphalt binder) were sought. Leached emissions of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) from asphalt shingles were utilized from Azah (2011) and adjusted for the asphalt content of asphalt shingles (i.e., 22% from US EPA (2015)) and the quantity of asphalt binder in kraft-faced fiberglass insulation (calculated as approximately 3%), assuming that the asphalt in shingles is responsible for the leaching of PAHs.

Gas emissions were estimated assuming the decomposition of the kraft paper facing used in faced fiberglass insulation.  The mass of kraft paper facing used in the average discarded US fiberglass insulation product was calculated using 2002 economic census data (USCB 2005) on the total sales value of major categories of fiberglass insulation products.

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the calculated, compacted density of fiberglass insulation with the compression factor calculated in IWCS (2017).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
Name: Use of clay brick; as soil fill
Description: This process models the placement of clay brick as a soil fill. This includes emissions from transporting of the material to the soil fill site, particulate emissions resulting from the dumping of the material at a stockpile site, emissions due to the placement and spreading of the bricks assuming placement is similar to riprap, and emissions due to the leaching of clay brick in a soil fill application. Some fraction of the material likely represents mortar affixed to bricks after demolition. Emissions from mortar, including leaching emissions, are not included in this data set.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
NAICS Subcategory: 5629: Remediation and Other Waste Management Services
Name: Mixed recyclables, recovered for material recycling facility (MRF)
Description:
This attributional unit process LCI represents the breakdown in percentages of sources of postconsumer material collected for material recycling facilities (MRFs). The process only represents the source mix percentages per 1 kg; no incoming transport is included in this process. Transport to the MRF and energy requirements for sorting are included in the next step in this linked model. The LCI study was conducted by Franklin Associates, Ltd. and sponsored jointly by NAPCOR, the American Chemistry Council (ACC), the Association of Postconsumer Plastic Recyclers (APR), and the PET Resin Association (PETRA). Using life cycle inventory (LCI) methodology, the study determines and quantifies the energy requirements, solid wastes, and atmospheric and waterborne emissions for the processes required to collect postconsumer PET and HDPE packaging, sort and separate the material, and reprocess it into clean recycled resin.

Important note: although most of the data in the US LCI database has undergone some sort of review, the database as a whole has not yet undergone a formal validation process. Please email comments to lci@nrel.gov.

NAICS Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services
NAICS Subcategory: 5629: Remediation and Other Waste Management Services
Name: Mixed recyclables, sorted, at material recycling facility (MRF)
Description:
This attributional unit process LCI represents the incoming transport of collected mixed recyclables to the material recycling facility (MRF) and energy requirements for the subsequent sorting and separation of recyclable materials; i.e., the sorting of plastics from other co-collected recovered materials (such as paper, steel, and aluminum), and separating mixed plastics into individual resins. The LCI study was conducted by Franklin Associates, Ltd. and sponsored jointly by NAPCOR, the American Chemistry Council (ACC), the Association of Postconsumer Plastic Recyclers (APR), and the PET Resin Association (PETRA). Using life cycle inventory (LCI) methodology, the study determines and quantifies the energy requirements, solid wastes, and atmospheric and waterborne emissions for the processes required to collect postconsumer PET and HDPE packaging, sort and separate the material, and reprocess it into clean recycled resin.

Important note: although most of the data in the US LCI database has undergone some sort of review, the database as a whole has not yet undergone a formal validation process. Please email comments to lci@nrel.gov.

Name: Asphalt mixture - Framework
Description:
This asphalt mixture process is the base case for the asphalt mixtures LCA.

This is a parameterized inventory. 
When using this please note:
- the paramter Total_amount_of_binder indicates the target amount = virgin binder + binder derived from RAP/RAS
- to define a mix, %RAP or %RAS and target asphalt binder %
- fuels for production at plant included in this inventory are: natural gas, diesel, propane and recycled oil, with default values included (Source: NAPA EPD program). Bio-diesel and residual fuel oil is also included in the parameter list, but there is no default value included. 
- the Time_of_* are always set to 1 year - and serve as a flag when selecting fuels. The default fuel is natural gas.
- four asphalt binder alternatives have been included: unmodified, and modified with SBS (actually SBR), PPA and GTR.
- all distances are in ton-miles - please ensure appropriate unit conversion when reporting these parameters.
- percentage by wt of additive - e.g. Evotherm is included, but currenlty there is no associated upstream inventory available for it. 
- electricity use is 2.78 kwh/sh-ton

The default parameters for the mixes discussed in the LCA report are:
 Baseline Mix: 30% RAP with AC contribution of 5%, target binder content 5%, RAP travel distance: 50 ton-miles, Aggregate travel distance: 21.5 ton-miles, Asphalt binder travel distance: 3.9 ton mile by train.
The 5% Virgin Mix: 0-35% RAP, 5% asphalt binder, RAP travel distance: varying from 0 to 50 ton-miles, Aggregate travel distance: 5.8 ton-miles, Asphalt binder travel distance: 3.9 ton mile by train.
The Null Mix: 5% asphalt binder, 0% RAP, all travel distances set to 0.

Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Ethylene glycol, materials production, organic compound, at plant, kg - RNA"
According to the National Library of Medicine, ethylene glycol is used to make antifreeze (see link below) and therefore is used here as a proxy for those inventories where "antifreeze" is an input flow, but who give no additional information on the composition of the antifreeze used.

https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/compound/Ethylene-Glycol

Name: Grease, from petroleum Bridge; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery".
As for motor oil (see link below), lubricating greases can be either synthetic, or made from refined petroleum.  (NAICS codes 325998 and 324191 respectively). Therefore, USLCI "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery" may only be a good proxy the latter kind of grease.
https://thelubepage.com/amsoil-magazine-articles/magazine-articles/articles/petroleum-oil-production-and-oil-vs-synthetic-motor-oil

Conversion of 874 kg/m3 is based on an API 30 oil, according to the link below.
https://www.machinerylubrication.com/Read/29319/measuring-relative-density#:~:text=The%20density%20of%20most%20oils,they%20are%20lighter%20by%20volume.

Name: Motor oil, from petroleum Bridge; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery".
Motor oil and lubricant may be seen as synonyms, but the fact that the USLCI flow is â€œat refineryâ€ and not â€œat plantâ€ would suggest USLCIâ€™s â€œLubricant feedstock, at refinery" is only a proxy for â€œMotor oil, from refined petroleum; at plantâ€.
In addition, this bridge process was created to help distinguish between â€œMotor oilâ€ and other petroleum derivates for whom â€œLubricant feedstock, at refineryâ€ may also be used as proxy.
Note that motor oils can be either synthetic, or made from refined petroleum (see link below).
https://thelubepage.com/amsoil-magazine-articles/magazine-articles/articles/petroleum-oil-production-and-oil-vs-synthetic-motor-oil

Name: Blasthole drill; Manufacture; For surface coal mine, at plant
Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This unit process encompasses the materials necessary to construct a single blasthole drill to drill holes for blasting at a large surface coal mine with dragline based on a P&H 250XP-DL Dragline Application Drill. Quantifies the amount of steel needed.

GOAL AND SCOPE
The scope of this process encompasses the materials and weights of those materials necessary to construct a single 250,000 lb blasthole drill, to be used for mining in a large scale surface mine. The process is based on the reference flow of 1 piece of blasthole drill, 250,000 lbs, as described below. The blasthole drill is assumed to be constructed entirely of steel; other materials are assumed to be negligible. By default, all steel within this study was assumed to be steel plate, based on available GaBi profiles, unless other steel types were specified per available data, or a higher grade of steel would be required, per NETL engineering judgment. Therefore, all steel considered in this unit process was assumed to be steel plate.

This process is used during LC Stage #1 to assist in the mining of coal from surface mines (such as Powder River Basin sub-bituminous coal). It is combined with other mining equipment construction unit processes in the assembly unit process, Coal mine equipment assembly; Production mix; Surface coal mine, Powder river basin, U.S. This assembly unit process quantifies the fraction of each piece of equipment needed under LC Stage #1 to produce 1 kg of coal ready for transport (LC Stage #2) to the energy conversion facility (LC Stage #3).

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
Construction of the blasthole drill is based on manufacturer specifications for a P&H 250XP-DL Dragline Application Drill, Rotary Blasthole Drill which weighs 250,000 lbs. The blasthole drill is used to facilitate removal of overburden and coal at the surface mine. The blasthole drill produces a series of holes, into which blasting powder is inserted and exploded, in order to loosen and facilitate overburden removal and coal extraction. This unit process considers only the construction materials required for the blasthole drill. Use of explosives is considered in a separate unit process.

An overview of the boundary of this unit process can be found in the Associated Documentation (referenced below). Emissions related to the physical assembly of the blasthole drill (e.g., that are emitted while putting together the components of a blasthole drill, including transport of those components) are not considered in this study. Upstream emissions from the production of raw materials used for the construction of the blasthole drill (e.g., steel plate) are calculated outside the boundary of this unit process, based on proprietary profiles available within the GaBi model. As discussed above, the blasthole drill constructed in this unit process is incorporated into the surface mine assembly processes for LC Stage #1 for surface mined Powder River Basin sub-bituminous coal.

The total weight of a blasthole drill was readily available but reliable data for the material breakdown of blasthole drill subcomponents were not. Therefore, the blasthole drill was assumed to be composed entirely of steel plate (Steel plate, BF (85% Recovery Rate) [Metals]). Total weight for one blasthole drill is estimated to be approximately 113,400 kg (250,000 lbs) (P&H 2009). Based on the assumption that the blasthole drill is constructed entirely out of steel plate, the total weight is assigned to this material. The associated documentation (referenced below) discusses: 1) relevant properties and assumptions used to calculate the amount of steel plate contained in a single blasthole drill; 2) a summary of modeled input and output flows; and 3) additional details regarding input and output flows, including calculation methods.

4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This unit process is a construction process that includes material requirements for the construction of a coal cleaning facility, which is a steel building used to house coal cleaning equipment. 

GOAL AND SCOPE
This parameterized unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the construction of a steel building to be used to house coal cleaning equipment; a steel building with a reinforced concrete slab as a foundation. This process encompasses all of the materials that are required for construction. Input flows include concrete cement, rebar wire, cold-rolled stainless steel, and zinc for the use of zinc-galvanized steel panels.  The reference flow of this unit process is: 1 piece (pcs) of Coal Cleaning Facility.

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
This process encompasses all of the materials that are required in the construction of a steel building to be used to house coal cleaning equipment; a steel building with a reinforced concrete slab as a foundation. The concrete slab will also serve as the first floor of the building. Carbon steel I-beams (high-strength, low alloy) will form the exterior frame of the buildings as well as each of the other five floors, plus the roof (Saginaw, 2009). The walls and roof of the facility will be constructed out of zinc-galvanized steel panels (Bay Area Rapid Transit, 2004; Buildings Direct, 2009; Steel Building, 2009; Engineers Edge, 2009). 

The exterior of the building will be 100 ft. long Ã— 60 ft. wide (NETL, 2010). Vertical I-beams will be spaced every 20 ft. along all four walls. Horizontal I-beams will form the floors from the second level up to and including the roof (six total levels). Each level will be 14 ft. tall, for a total building height of 84 ft (NETL, 2010). The I-beams for the floor will be spaced every 20 ft., running horizontally both the length and width of the building. The weight of the steel for the I-beams will be determined by taking the average of four beams with a depth of 12 in.
The exterior of the building, all four walls and the roof, will be clad in 26-gauge zinc-galvanized carbon steel panels (Buildings Direct, 2009; Engineers Edge, 2009; Steel Building, 2009). The zinc is a layer with a volume of approximately 1.25 ounces per square ft (oz/ft2) of steel panel (Bay Area Rapid Transit, 2004).

The foundation of the building, which will also serve as the ground floor, will be constructed using a 4,000 psi mix of concrete (NETL, 2010). The concrete foundation will be reinforced by size #4 carbon steel rebar running horizontally over both the length and the width of the concrete. The rebar will be evenly spaced every 12 in. in both directions, and has a cross-sectional area of 0.20 in2 (CRSI, 2008). The perimeter of the slab must be located below the frost line, which is located 21 in. below ground level at the mine location (Weather Bureau, 2008). The exterior of the concrete slab will extend an additional 6 in. below the frost line and will be 18 in. thick around the perimeter. The interior of the concrete slab will be 1 in. thick.

The flooring for each level of the building is assumed to consist of steel grating. This weight is excluded from these calculations due to the assumption they will be balanced by the sections of the interior I-beams that will be removed for stairways and to accommodate large pieces of equipment.

The weight of each material used in the coal cleaning facility was calculated separately. The materials were cold-rolled steel for the I-beams and the exterior paneling, zinc for the galvanization of the paneling, reinforcement bar (i.e. rebar) for reinforcing the concrete foundation, and concrete for the building foundation. The input and output flows for the construction of the coal cleaning facility are illustrated in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

This process contains one or more surrogate product flows as replacements for proprietary product flows used in the NETL Unit Process Library.  All surrogate flow were taken from the U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database; a list of surrogate flows used in this process is provided below:
1) Cold rolled sheet, steel, at plant
2) Portland cement, at plant: This flow is a proxy for Ready Mixed Concrete.  This process calculates the required mass of Portland Cement as a fraction of the required mass of Ready Mixed Concrete to construct one coal cleaning facility.  The default parameter setting assumes that Portland Cement comprises 14.1% (by mass) of Ready Mixed Concrete (NIST 2009); however, this parameter may be modified by the user.  This process does not consider other material and energy inputs for producing Ready Mixed Concrete.
3) Zinc, sheet

Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
The amount of electricity required to power equipment used for cleaning coal at underground and surface mines as well as accounting for coal loss during production activities.

GOAL AND SCOPE
This unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the amount of electricity required to power equipment used for cleaning coal at an underground mine. A centrifuge, flotation machine, screens, and magnetic separator are the pieces of equipment used to clean coal. The process also accounts for the solid wastes that are incurred during the production and processing steps to scale upstream activities for one kg of coal at the entrance to the RMT gate. Inputs are electricity, coal, and the water use and quality associated with 1 kg of cleaned coal. All of these items are also adjustable parameters to measure uncertainties. Cleaned coal and the course and fine wastes are the outputs. The unit process is based on the reference flow of one kg of coal. The relevant flows of this unit process are described below and shown the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
A visual representation of the boundary of this unit process is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).  Coal cleaning is performed to separate coal from other materials (rock, dirt, clay, etc.) contained in run-of-mine (raw) coal (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association, 2002). The amount and type of materials to be removed is dependent on the particular coal seam being mined, and the forms of that removed material (course or fine slurry wastes) are dependent on the type of coal cleaning processes that are used.

The cleaning process and energy requirements are based on the data provided in Chapter 2 of the Energy and Environmental Profile of the U.S. Mining Industry developed by the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy division and National Mining Association (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association, 2002). The process therein serves as a generic coal preparation facility for an eastern U.S. underground mine and contains a centrifuge, flotation machine, screens, and magnetic separator. BCS, Incorporated (BCS) developed the data by integrating the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Information Administrationâ€™s 1997 Coal Industry data into the Western Mining Engineering, Inc.â€™s SHERPA Mine Cost Software to create 2002 estimates. BCS also used Mine and Mill Equipment Cost, An Estimatorâ€™s Guide to develop the data.

It is assumed that all equipment uses electricity, and the electricity requirement was calculated by dividing the sum of the equipmentsâ€™ energy requirements, in Btu per ton, by the appropriate conversions to obtain the electricity needed in kWh per kg of cleaned coal (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association, 2002). It is also assumed that these energy requirements are applicable to processing coal from a surface mine. The electricity factor is an adjustable parameter in the DS file, so the item could be adjusted to measure uncertainty. One key piece of information missing in the discussion of the energy requirements is how much solid waste is generated from processing the coal. Figure 2-3 of the report shows that waste is 15 percent of the material produced in mining (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association, 2002). Due to lack of specific information, it is assumed that the energy requirements for coal cleaning are based on a run-of-mine coal containing 15 percent waste. The unit process will scale the electricity usage according to the amount of waste produced relative to the assumed 15 percent baseline.

The default course and fine waste flows are based on a â€œrealisticâ€ process for Illinois No. 6 coal (Bhagwat, 2009). As mentioned above, the amount of waste generated by a specific facility is highly depended on the source of the coal, so data for a specific facility should be used if at all possible, particularly if life cycle results are sensitive to the results of this process. If no other data is available it is recommended that the 15 percent baseline value is used, which is provided by the parameter base_scaler. These flows should be sent to processes to either account for the burdens to dispose of them or make use of the products, such as concrete manufacturing.

The amount of raw coal input to the process is based on producing 1 kg of cleaned coal, thus it is the sum of: 1 kg of cleaned coal and the course and fine waste flows.

Water use and emissions are accounted for separately in a Water Use and Quality unit process, which is linked through the input flow, Coal, preparation, water [Intermediate flow]. The input flow is statically defined as 1 kg to match the reference flow of this unit process of 1 kg cleaned coal; therefore, the upstream water use and quality unit process should use the same reference flow (i.e., input and output flows scaled to 1 kg of cleaned coal rather than raw coal input).

This unit process parameterizes key variables, which allow for evaluation of data uncertainty when used in a life cycle model.  Additional details regarding input and output flows, including calculation methods, are contained in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

This process contains one or more surrogate product flows as replacements for proprietary product flows used in the NETL Unit Process Library.  All surrogate flow were taken from the U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database; a list of surrogate flows used in this process is provided below:
1) Electricity, at Grid, US, 2010

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This parameterized unit process is based on manufacturer's specifications, a conveyor system, 1.83 m (72 in) width, from Sandvik to transport coal from prep facility to loading silo at a Powder River Basin coal mine. Materials include cold-rolled steel, hot-dip galvanized steel, and rubber.

GOAL AND SCOPE
The scope of this unit process covers the materials required for the construction of a single conveyor system with steel cords needed to transport Powder River Basin sub-bituminous coal from the preparation facility to the loading silo, where the coal is loaded into railcars and transported to the energy conversion facility. The process is based on the reference flow of 1 piece (pcs) of steel-cord conveyor system, as described below and shown in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). The steel-cord conveyor system is assumed to be constructed of cold-rolled steel, hot-dip galvanized steel, and rubber; quantities of other material types are assumed to be negligible.

This construction unit process for the steel-cord conveyor system is combined with other pieces of equipment used during coal preparation on site, under Life Cycle (LC) Stage #1, in an individual assembly unit process, Coal mine equipment assembly; Production mix; Surface coal mine, Powder river basin, U.S. This assembly unit process quantifies the fraction of each piece of equipment needed under LC Stage #1 to produce 1 kg of Powder River Basin coal ready for transport (LC Stage #2) to the energy conversion facility (LC Stage #3).

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
A visual representation of the boundary of this unit process is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). Emissions related to the physical assembly of the steel-cord conveyor system (e.g., emitted while putting together the components of the conveyor system, including transport of those components) are not considered in this study. Upstream emissions from the production of raw materials used for the construction of the steel-cord conveyor system (e.g., steels) are calculated outside the boundary of this unit process, based on proprietary profiles available in the GaBi model. As discussed above and shown in the associated documentation (referenced below), the steel-cord conveyor system constructed in this unit process is incorporated into the construction assembly for LC Stage #1.

The total weight of a steel-cord conveyor system was readily available, but reliable data for the material breakdown of steel-cord conveyor system were not. The steel-cord conveyor system is designed such that it transports the sized coal from the preparation facility to the loading silo. The specifications found for the conveyor system included information for the cold-rolled steel , hot-dip galvanized steel, and rubber.

Relevant properties and assumptions used to calculate the amount of steel plate contained in a single steel-cord conveyor system are given in the associated documentation (referenced below). Total weight for one steel-cord conveyor system was calculated to be approximately 1,064,007 kg (1,172 short tons).  The associated documentation also contains additional information regarding: modeled input and output flows and calculation methods.

Name: Coal conveyor system; Manufacture; For underground mine, at point-of-sale; 1.22 m belt width
Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION					
This parameterized unit process encompasses the materials necessary for the construction of one steel cord conveyor belt with rubber facing (1.21 m wide), pulleys, and idlers based on manufacturersâ€™ specifications for a 1.22 m (48 inches) conveyor system.

GOAL AND SCOPE
The scope of this unit process encompasses the materials and weights of those materials necessary to construct a single, 1.22 m (48 in) conveyor system, used to carry coal from an underground coal mine to the surface during extraction of Illinois No. 6 bituminous coal. The unit process is based on the reference flow of 1 piece of conveyor system, 1.22 m (48 in), as described below and shown in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). The conveyor system is assumed to be constructed of cold-rolled steel, hot-dip galvanized steel, and natural rubber. Other materials are assumed to be negligible.

This unit process is combined with other coal mine equipment construction unit processes in an assembly unit process for Illinois No. 6 bituminous coal, Coal mine equipment assembly; Production mix; Underground coal mine, Illinois No. 6 Coal, U.S. The assembly unit process quantifies the fraction of each piece of equipment needed under Life Cycle (LC) Stage #1 to produce 1 kg of Illinois No. 6 bituminous coal ready for transport (LC Stage #2).  See the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below) for more information.

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
Construction of the conveyor system is based on manufacturer specifications for a single conveyor system, having a 1,554.48-m long and 1.2192 m-wide steel-cord belt (Roberts & Schaefer Company 2007). Extraction of coal biomass from the Illinois No. 6 bituminous underground coal mine requires conveyor systems, which are used to carry coal from underground longwall mine operation to a coal stockpile on the surface during the coal extraction process.

An overview of the boundary of this unit process can be found in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). Emissions related to the physical assembly of the conveyor system (e.g., emitted while assembling and transporting conveyor components) are not included in the study boundary. As shown in the associated documentation, the conveyor system constructed in this unit process is incorporated into the coal mine construction assembly processes for LC Stage #1 for coal biomass.

The major components of the conveyor belt system are the conveyor frame, the brackets on which the belt is carried, the rotating parts (rollers) on which the belt travels, the drive and tail pulleys that move the belt, the steel cord inside the belt, and the rubber compound that surrounds the belt cord and provides a surface to carry loaded coal. There are two individual idler systems in this conveyor system: the main idlers that carry the belt and the coal from the mine to the surface, and a return idler system that loops below the idlers and carries the belt back to the underground mine.

The total weight of major components in a conveyor system is readily available but reliable data for the materials breakdown of subcomponents were not. Therefore, the conveyor system was assumed to be composed of three main materials: cold-rolled steel, hot-dip galvanized steel, and natural rubber. The life cycle emissions of raw materials used for the construction of the conveyor system (e.g., cold-rolled steel) are calculated outside the boundary of this unit process, based on proprietary profiles available within the life cycle inventory (LCI) databases.

Relevant properties and assumptions used to calculate the amount of materials used to construct the conveyor system can be found in the associated documentation. The idler system used is a three-roll, inline, carry-and-impact system. The rollers have a diameter of 152 millimeters (mm) and a bearing size of 6,306. There are two separate aspects to the idler system: the rotating parts (rollers), which are manufactured from cold-rolled steel, and the frame and brackets, which are manufactured from hot-dip galvanized steel. These two aspects of the conveyor system weigh a combined 58.3 kilograms (kg). The rotating parts make up 24.3 kg, while the frame and brackets make up 34.0 kg (Sandvik 2004). The idler systems are assumed to be spaced one meter apart along the entire length of the conveyor belt.

The return idler system consists of a single roller manufactured from cold-rolled steel, and the frame and brackets, which are manufactured from galvanized steel. The rollers have a diameter of 152 mm and a bearing size of 6,306. The return idler system weighs 33.9 kg, with the rotating parts being 20.6 kg and the frame and brackets being 13.3 kg (Sandvik 2004). The return idler systems are assumed to be spaced every 3 meters, running along the same path as the idler systems. Based on this assumption, the rotating parts weigh 6.87 kg/m and the frame and brackets weigh 4.43 kg/m.

This conveyor belt requires both a drive pulley to power the belt and a tail pulley for the belt to return to the mine to carry more coal to the surface. Both the drive and tail pulleys are assumed to be constructed of 100 percent cold rolled steel. The tail pulley has a weight of 1,023 kg, and the drive pulley weighs 1,044 kg (Sandvik 2003).

The conveyor belt consists of hot-dip galvanized-steel cords of a nominal 5.2-mm diameter running the entire length of the belt (Goodyear Engineered Products 2008a and 2008b). Based on manufacturersâ€™ instructions, the cords are assumed to be spaced every 11.4 mm on center and for each, seven thin steel cables were grouped together; seven bundles of these cables were then wound together to form a 7x7 cord. The nearest representative cable diameter was 3/16â€, or approximately 4.76 mm, and each cord has a weight of approximately 9.23 kg/100 m, which was converted to 0.0923 kg/m (Lexco 2006, Loos & Co. 2009). Spread evenly over the entire 1.2192-m width of the belt, there will be 106 cords running the length of the belt. To get the weight of the steel cord per meter of belt, the weight of a single cord (0.0923 kg/m) was multiplied by the number of cords based on the belt width for a value of 9.78 kg/m.

The total weight of the conveyor belt was taken from Goodyearâ€™s Flexsteel Belt (Goodyear Engineered Products 2008b). The belt is constructed of a series of steel cords surrounded by an insulating layer of rubber, which is itself surrounded by a top and bottom cover of rubber. A diagram is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

The top rubber cover is assumed to be 4.5-mm thick and the bottom cover 1.5-mm thick. The nominal diameter of the steel cables is 5.2 mm, for a total belt thickness of 11.2 mm. The belt weight is the cover weight (7.5 kg/m2) plus the carcass weight (14.6 kg/m2), which comes out to 22.1 kg/m2. The weight of the belt was multiplied by the width of the belt, 1.2192 m, to obtain a value of 26.94 kg/m of belt. The weight of steel cord was subtracted from this value, for a total of 17.16 kg of rubber per meter of belt. (Goodyear Engineered Products 2008b).

The materials manufactured from hot-dip galvanized steel include the steel cord, the idler frame and brackets, and the return idler frame and brackets. The steel cord weighs 9.78 kg/m, the idler frame 34.0 kg/m, and the return idler frame 4.43 kg/m. Summed, this gives a total of 48.21 kg/m of hot-dip galvanized steel.

The third material in the conveyor system is cold-rolled steel for the idler and return idler rotating parts, the tail pulley, and the drive pulley. Since there is only one of each type of pulley, their weights will be added to the total cold-rolled steel value at the end of the calculations. The idler rotating parts weigh 24.3 kg/m, while the return idler rotating parts weigh 6.87 kg/m â€“ a total of 31.17 kg/m of cold-rolled steel. The idlers are assumed to be spaced 1 meter apart, and the return idlers are assumed to be spaced 3 meters apart.

Finally, the weight of each material was multiplied by the length of the belt to obtain a total weight. The belt has a given length of 1,554.48 m, which was doubled to 3,108.96 m to account for the fact that the belt actually makes a large loop to return to the underground mine. The total weight of rubber for the belt was 53,362.52 kg. The weight for hot-dip galvanized steel was 149,893.99 kg. The weight of cold-rolled steel was 31.17 kg/m times 3,108.96 m added to the weights of the drive and tail pulleys, for a total weight of 98,962.92 kg. A summary of modeled input and output flows and additional details regarding those flows, including calculation methods, are contained in the associated documentation.

This process contains one or more surrogate product flows as replacements for proprietary product flows used in the NETL Unit Process Library.  All surrogate flow were taken from the U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database; a list of surrogate flows used in this process is provided below:
1) Acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene copolymer, resin, at plant
2) Cold rolled sheet, steel, at plant
3) Galvanized steel sheet, at plant

NAICS Subcategory: Bit. Coal and Lignite Surface Mining
Name: Coal crusher facility; Construction; At underground mine
Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This parameterized unit process encompasses the total amount of materials used in the construction of a crushing facility and the coal crusher.

GOAL AND SCOPE
The scope of this unit process encompasses the materials and weights of those materials necessary to construct a single coal crusher facility, to be used for the preparation of underground Illinois No. 6 coal. The unit process is based on the reference flow of 1 coal crusher facility, as described below, and as shown in the associated documentation (referenced below). The crusher facility is assumed to be constructed of steel plate, reinforcement bar (rebar) wire and ready mixed concrete. Other materials are assumed to be negligible.  This unit process is used during LC Stage #1 to assist in the preparation of underground coal. It is combined with other mine equipment construction unit processes in the coal preparation facility assembly unit process for underground coal, Coal mine equipment assembly; Production mix; Underground coal mine, Illinois No. 6 Coal. This assembly unit process quantifies the fraction of each piece of equipment needed under LC Stage #1 to produce 1 kg of coal ready for transport (LC Stage #2) to the energy conversion facility (LC Stage #3).

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
The electricity requirements for coal beneficiation of a generic eastern coal mine are reported in Chapter 2, Table 2-7 of the EIA report â€œEnergy and Environmental Profile of the U.S. Mining Industryâ€(DOE and NMA 2002).  Beneficiation refers to the sizing, handling and washing of run-of-mine (ROM) coal. The energy consumption values reported for coal crushing (or grinding as it is referred to in the EIA report) is 93,200 Btu/ton of coal, and is one of the more energy intensive processes during coal mining.  When converted to metric units, coal crushing consumes 30.1 KWh/ton of coal processed.  The electricity requirements for the other phases of beneficiation are included in the coal cleaning section of this report. 

Coal mining accounts for approximately 30 percent of methane (CH4) emissions in the U.S., with underground mines contributing the largest share of coal mining CH4 emissions (EPA 2008b).  During coal acquisition, CH4 is released during both the underground coal extraction and the post mining coal preparation activities.  From the EPA inventory of greenhouse gas emissions from 1990-2006, 90% of CH4 emitted from underground mining is a result of coal extraction, with the remaining 10% emitted during preparation (EPA 2008b). 

In a report identifying opportunities for methane recovery from U.S. coal plants, the U.S. EPA Coalbed Methane outreach program surveyed methane emissions from 50 mines in 12 states, including the Galatia mine in Illinois(EPA 2008a).  Data from the Galatia mine is used throughout this study due to its similarities to the studied mine, and therefore Galatia methane values are also used. Data are summarized in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

In 2006, 13 coal mines had invested in degasification systems to collect CH4. Collecting CH4 reduced the overall greenhouse gas emissions from the mine, and the CH4 can then be used on- site or sold to a pipeline for energy generation (EPA 2008b).  This technique is especially cost-effective in gassy mines.  Coal mines in Illinois are generally less gassy than other regions, and to date no CH4 recovery systems have been implemented in an Illinois mine (EPA 2008a).  Therefore for this study, it is assumed that no CH4 recovery system is in place and all 360 ft3 CH4/ton coal is emitted.

As previously mentioned, 90 percent of emission are from coal extraction while 10 percent are from post mining activities. Due to a lack of more specific data, all post mining CH4 emissions have been allocated to the coal crushing operation.  As defined in Section X, this study considers the use of both longwall and continuous miners for coal extraction.  The EIA Annual Coal Report states that a typical longwall mining operation performs longwall 80 percent of the time and uses continuous mining for the remaining 20 percent (EIA 2007).  Therefore, the remaining 90 percent of CH4 emissions is allocated 80 percent to longwall and 20 percent to continuous operations. A table outlining the allocation of underground mining CH4 emissions is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). As a check, these values were summed to insure that the allocated emissions totaled the overall value of 360 ft3 CH4/ton coal. 

This process contains one or more surrogate product flows as replacements for proprietary product flows used in the NETL Unit Process Library.  All surrogate flow were taken from the U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database; a list of surrogate flows used in this process is provided below:
1) Portland cement, at plant: This flow is a proxy for Ready Mixed Concrete.  This process calculates the required mass of Portland Cement as a fraction of the required mass of Ready Mixed Concrete to construct one coal crusher facility.  The default parameter setting assumes that Portland Cement comprises 14.1% (by mass) of Ready Mixed Concrete (NIST 2009); however, this parameter may be modified by the user.  This process does not consider other material and energy inputs for producing Ready Mixed Concrete.

NAICS Subcategory: Bit. Coal and Lignite Surface Mining
Name: Coal extraction; Surface mine
Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the extraction of coal from a surface mine. This includes the amount of electricity and fuel required to power equipment and the direct particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions from operating equipment and using explosives. Combustion emissions are not included in this unit process.


MODELING NOTE
In the DS file, this unit process is one of three mining stages grouped into a single proces entitled, "Surface Coal Mining â€“ Overburden Removal, Extraction, and Reclamation".  The metadata below reflects the Goal and Scope, Boundary and Description, and Citation and Associated Documentation of all three mining stages within the combined process.

GOAL AND SCOPE
This unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the extraction of coal from a surface mine. This includes the amount of electricity and fuel required to power equipment and the direct particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions from operating equipment and using explosives. Combustion emissions are not included in this unit process. The reference flow of this unit process is: 1 kg of coal.

Note: This process deviates from the DS in that it uses the input "Ammonia, steam reforming, liquid, at plant" in place of "Ammonium nitrate".  The input value for ammonia reflects the amount of ammonia necessary to produce the required quantity of ammonium nitrate for this process.


BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
A visual representation of the boundary of this unit process is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). 	This UP is parameterized into mining stages: Overburden removal, coal extraction, and mine reclamation. This UP is based on a reference Western mine. A strip ratio from any mine can be applied to scale the impacts of the overburden removal scenario. The reference strip ratio is 5:1 and represents a reference Western mine. This UP has a diesel scalar and electricity switch to account for mines that use all diesel equipment (e.g., Indonesia).

For energy calculations, the mining equipment was separated into electrically powered equipment and diesel powered equipment. For all calculations, the equipment was separated into mining stage: overburden removal, coal extraction, and mine reclamation.

The diesel requirements were calculated using the Energy and Environmental Profile of the U.S. Mining Industry developed by the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy division and the National Mining Association (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association, n.d.).  Chapter Two of the source focused on coal; description of coal types, overview of coal mining, energy requirements for underground and surface mines, and emissions from coal mining.  The data source provides energy data for specific mining activities such as coal handling, extraction, and grinding.  BCS, Incorporated (BCS) developed the data by integrating the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Information Administrationâ€™s 1997 Coal Industry data into the Western Mining Engineering, Inc.â€™s SHERPA Mine Cost Software to create 2002 estimates.  BCS also used Mine and Mill Equipment Cost, An Estimatorâ€™s Guide to develop the data.  Newer data for specific coal mining processes are not available.  This unit process parameterizes key variables to allow for evaluation of data uncertainty when used in a life cycle model.

The reference flow for this unit process is one kg of coal.  To calculate the amount of diesel needed, the sum of the equipmentâ€™s energy requirements, in Btu per ton, was divided by the appropriate conversion to convert tons to kg and then divided by the high heating value of diesel (U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2011), in Btu per kg, to obtain the diesel requirement in kg of diesel per kg of coal.  

To calculate the amount of ammonium nitrate and fuel oil (ANFO) used to excavate coal, 1994 data specific to the Powder River Basin (PRB) was used (Stump, B., 1995).  The average weight percentages for the composition of ANFO were taken from an ANFO material safety data sheet (Maxam North America, 2010).  The amount of ammonium nitrate was calculated by multiplying the kg of ANFO per coal produced by the average weight percent of ammonium nitrate.  The kg of ANFO per coal produced was multiplied by the average weight percent of light fuel oil to calculate the amount of light fuel oil in kg fuel oil per kg of coal.  Particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are emitted during coal extraction from an underground mine.  To calculate the amount of VOC emissions released during the excavation of coal, 1989-1998 data specific to the PRB (McVehil, G.E., 2001) and 2011 coal production data by the National Mining Association (National Mining Association, 2012) was used. The amount of VOCs released, in tons per year, was multiplied by the appropriate conversion and divided by the amount of coal produced, which was also multiplied by the appropriate conversions, to obtain the kg of VOC per kg of coal.  The VOC emissions were calculated for the coal types of bituminous, subbituminous, and lignite by using the calculation just described above but replacing the coal produced with the appropriate coal type production value.  It was assumed the VOC emissions released were for one year.

The PM emissions were calculated using the U.S. Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s AP 42 (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 2009).  Chapter 11.9 of the source focused on Western surface coal which included emission factor equations and scaling factors.  Other factors that were incorporated into these equations, such as coal moisture content, were obtained from a 2012 U.S. Department of Energy document (U.S. Department of Energy, 2012) and a Caterpillar, Inc. equipment specification (Caterpillar).  The PM emissions were broken down into extraction processes such as blasting, truck loading, bulldozing, etc.  Emission factor equations and conversions were used to calculate the amount of PM emissions based on the reference flow of one kg of coal.  Where applicable, the moisture content of coal was used to calculate PM emissions for the coal types of bituminous, subbituminous, and lignite.  To obtain the emissions for PM10 and PM2.5, the scaling factor pertaining to each emission was multiplied by the amount of PM emissions per unit of coal for both PM10 and PM25.  It was assumed that PM data not pertaining to coal types was calculated for a surface mine only.  For the bulldozer, it was assumed that the equipment operated for 12 hours per day.  A five miles per hour grading speed and 20 year lifetime of coal mine for existing mines was assumed in the calculation of the PM emissions for grading.  To calculate the unmitigated PM emissions, the sum of the PM10 and PM2.5 emissions were both multiplied by 15 percent, because it was assumed mitigation measures would reduce emissions by 85 percent.  The ammonium nitrate, electricity, light fuel oil, PM, and VOC requirements were placed as parameters in the DS file, so the items could be adjusted to measure uncertainties.  There are other adjustable parameters such as coal type and moisture content.

Additional details regarding input and output flows, including calculation methods, are contained in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

1) Diesel, at refinery
2) Electricity, at Grid, US, 2010

Name: Coal extraction; Underground mine
Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€ 
3) From the library, the unit process of interest can be found either through the "Complete Listing of the Unit Process Library link" or through the link to the "Unit Process Database" spreadsheet
4) For each process, a Data Sheet (DS) and Data File (DF) are available

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This parameterized unit process calculates the fuel and non-combustion emissions for the extraction of raw coal from an underground coal mine. Inputs and outputs are reported on the basis of 1 kg of coal.

GOAL AND SCOPE
This unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the amount of electricity required to power equipment used in the extraction of raw coal from an underground mine. This equipment includes drills and explosives loaders. Fuel and non-combustion emissions for the operation of an underground coal mine are also included. Key inputs include electricity, light fuel oil, ammonium nitrate, diesel, coal, coal mine methane, and coal ventilation. Electricity, light fuel oil, ammonium nitrate, and diesel are also adjustable parameters to measure uncertainties. Extracted coal, Dust (PM10 and PM2.5), and VOCs are key outputs. All of these outputs except extracted coal are also adjustable parameters to measure uncertainties. The unit process is based on the reference flow of one kg of coal. The relevant flows of this unit process are described below and shown the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

Note: This process deviates from the DS in that it uses the input "Ammonia, steam reforming, liquid, at plant" in place of "Ammonium nitrate".  The input value for ammonia reflects the amount of ammonia necessary to produce the required quantity of ammonium nitrate for this process.


BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
A visual representation of the boundary of this unit process is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). The electricity and diesel requirements were calculated using the Energy and Environmental Profile of the U.S. Mining Industry developed by the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy division and the National Mining Association (DOE and NMA 2002). Chapter Two of the source focused on coal; description of coal types, overview of coal mining, energy requirements for underground and surface mines, and emissions from coal mining. The data source provides energy data for specific mining activities such as coal handling, extraction, and grinding. BCS, Incorporated (BCS) developed the data by integrating the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Information Administrationâ€™s 1997 Coal Industry data into the Western Mining Engineering, Inc.â€™s SHERPA Mine Cost Software to create 2002 estimates. BCS also used Mine and Mill Equipment Cost, An Estimatorâ€™s Guide to develop the data. Newer data for specific coal mining processes are not available. This unit process parameterizes key variables, which allow for evaluation of data uncertainty when used in a life cycle model.

Drills, two boom jumbo, continuous mining machine, raise borers, ANFO loaders, roof bolter, water pumps, and diamond drill were the pieces of equipment used for extracting coal from an underground mine; the drills, two boom jumbo, continuous mining machine, water pumps, and diamond drill use electricity, while the raise borers, ANFO loaders, and roof bolter use diesel. The reference flow for this process is one kg of coal. To calculate the electricity needed, the sum of the energy requirements for the equipment that uses electricity, in Btu per ton, was divided by the appropriate conversions to obtain the electricity needed in kWh per kg. The diesel requirement was calculated by dividing the sum of the energy requirements, in Btu per ton, for the equipment that uses diesel by the appropriate conversion to convert tons to kg and then divided by the high heating value of diesel (EIA 2011), in Btu per kg, to obtain the diesel requirement in kg of diesel per kg of coal. To calculate the amount of ANFO used to excavate coal, 1994 data specific to the Powder River Basin (PRB) was used (Stump 1995). The average weight percentages for the composition of ammonium nitrate and fuel oil in ANFO were taken from an ANFO material safety data sheet (Maxam North America 2010). The amount of ammonium nitrate required, in kg ammonium nitrate per kg coal, was calculated by multiplying the kg of ANFO per coal produced by the average weight percent of ammonium nitrate. The kg of ANFO per coal produced was multiplied by the average weight percent of light fuel oil to calculate the amount of light fuel oil required in kg fuel oil per kg of coal. Particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are emitted during coal extraction from an underground mine. To calculate the amount of VOC emissions released during the excavation of coal, 1989-1998 data specific to the PRB (McVehil, Addison, and Baugues 2001) and 2011 coal production data by the National Mining Association (NMA 2009) was used. In National Mining Association (2009), the total value for each coal type was used as the coal production item in the calculations. The amount of VOCs released, in tons per year, was multiplied by the appropriate conversion and divided by the amount of coal produced, which was also multiplied by the appropriate conversions, to obtain kg of VOC per kg of coal. The VOC emissions were calculated for the coal types of bituminous, subbituminous, and lignite by using the calculation just described above but replacing the coal produced with the appropriate coal type production value. It was assumed the VOC emissions released were for one year. Data for PM emissions was taken from 2008 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency data (EPA n.d.). Underground minesâ€™ coal production data was obtained from 2008 U.S. Energy Information Administration historical data (EIA n.d.). PM emissions were calculated for underground mines only, not specific coal types. The PM emission factors were separated into PM10 and PM2.5. The following description for calculating the PM10 emissions is the same for PM2.5. The amount of PM10 emissions for each county throughout the U.S. was multiplied by the coal production of its specific county to obtain the amount of PM10 emissions within the specific county. This number was then multiplied by the coal production percentage for the specific county, which was the coal production for the specific county divided by the total coal production throughout all the U.S. counties in order to obtain the emissions for the specific county. The PM10 and PM2.5 emissions are the sum of the PM10 and PM2.5 emissions for every county, respectively. A minimum and maximum value for PM10 and PM25 was obtained. The PM10 minimum and maximum values were calculated by taking the percentile of the PM10 emissions per coal production for each county for 25 percent and 75 percent, respectively. This method was also used to calculate the minimum and maximum values for PM2.5. The ammonium nitrate, electricity, light fuel oil, PM, and VOC requirements were placed as parameters so the items could be adjusted to measure uncertainties.

Additional details regarding input and output flows, including calculation methods, are contained in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

BRIEF DESCRIPTION
This parameterized unit process calculates the amount of electricity and diesel required to power a coal conveyor, service truck, and load haul dumper for an underground mine.

GOAL AND SCOPE
This unit process provides a summary of relevant input and output flows associated with the amount of electricity and diesel required to power a coal conveyor, service trucks, and load haul dumper. All pieces of equipment are used to transport coal from an underground mine. The key inputs are electricity, diesel, and extracted coal, while coal is the key output. The unit process is based on the reference flow of one kg of coal.  The relevant flows of this unit process are described below and shown the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

BOUNDARY AND DESCRIPTION
A visual representation of the boundary of this unit process is available in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below). The electricity and diesel requirements were calculated using the Energy and Environmental Profile of the U.S. Mining Industry developed by the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy division and the National Mining Association (U.S. Department of Energy and National Mining Association). Chapter Two of the source focused on coal; description of coal types, overview of coal mining, energy requirements for underground and surface mines, and emissions from coal mining. The data source provides energy data for specific mining activities such as coal handling, extraction, and grinding. BCS, Incorporated (BCS) developed the data by integrating the U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Energy Information Administrationâ€™s 1997 Coal Industry data into the Western Mining Engineering, Inc.â€™s SHERPA Mine Cost Software to create 2002 estimates. BCS also used Mine and Mill Equipment Cost, An Estimatorâ€™s Guide to develop the data. Newer data for specific coal mining processes are not available. This unit process parameterizes key variables, which allow for evaluation of data uncertainty when used in a life cycle model.

A conveyor, load haul dumper, and service trucks were the pieces of equipment used for coal handling at an underground mine; the conveyor and load haul dumper are powered by electricity, while the service trucks use diesel. To calculate the electricity requirement (in kWh per kg of coal), the sum of the conveyor and load haul dumper energy requirements, in Btu per ton, was divided by the appropriate conversions to determine the electricity needed on the basis of one kg of coal, which is the reference flow. No coal losses were assumed. To obtain the diesel requirement, on the basis of the reference flow, the energy requirement for the service trucks was divided by the appropriate conversion to convert tons to kg and then divided by the high heating value of diesel (U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2011), in Btu per kg, to obtain the diesel requirement in kg of diesel per kg of coal. It was assumed that vehicle efficiency was included in the diesel energy requirements. The electricity and diesel requirements were placed as parameters in the DS file, so the items could be adjusted to measure uncertainties.

Additional details regarding input and output flows, including calculation methods, are contained in the associated documentation (referenced in the Citation and Associated Documentation section below).

1) Electricity, at Grid, US, 2010

Description:
This process is part of the NETL Unit Process Library.  To access the library and the original dataset:
1) Navigate to the NETL LCA home page (http://www.netl.doe.gov/lca)
2) Then navigate to the â€œUnit Process Libraryâ€
